data.title: ARGT: Lifting Of Currency Controls Paves The Way For The Argentine Economic Miracle
data.description:
Finally, after several months of negotiations, Argentine Economy Minister Luis Caputo announced this Friday a long-awaited agreement between Argentina and the International Monetary Fund (IMF) for an amount of $20 billion, of which approximately $12 billion will arrive as early as next week for the Argentine government to use freely. This far exceeded the consensus of analysts, who estimated that the initial IMF disbursement could be between $8 billion and $10 billion.

With the IMF agreement under its belt and the possibility of obtaining other important monetary support, the Milei government now intends to cancel the outstanding debt of the Central Bank (BCRA) that continued to generate the issuance of pesos through interest payments, as well as the lifting, starting this Monday, of the exchange controls that were still in force in the country since 2019 (cepo).

After a stellar year, Argentine stocks are under strong pressure in the first months of 2025 amid a tariff war between the United States and China that has plunged global financial markets into uncertainty, severely impacting valuations. So far this year, the Argentine equity and ADR ETF, Global X MSCI Argentina ETF (NYSEARCA:ARGT), has seen a significant correction in search of new growth catalysts and as a result of profit-taking by investors.

Despite this, the fundamentals of the Argentine economy remain solid, now marking a very important step in the implementation of the pro-market agenda of the government led by President Javier Milei. But let me first explain what's going on in Argentina and why this will be relevant in the coming years.

Macro Context

Under the exchange control regime officially implemented in 2011, during the administration of Cristina Fernández de Kirchner, with a brief interruption between 2015 and 2019 during Mauricio Macri's term, the exchange controls acted as a restriction on the free availability of dollars in the economy by the population, limiting the amount of U.S. dollars a person could purchase on the regulated official market to $200 per month.

This made both savings and transactions in this currency extremely difficult, fostering the proliferation of a black market for the sale of dollars and creating an exchange rate gap that demonstrated the price distortions prevailing in the economy. In turn, companies wishing to import from abroad or distribute profits and dividends in dollars to their foreign shareholders had to request prior authorization from the State to sell them dollars. Under this restriction, foreign companies wishing to invest in the country could not compensate their shareholders abroad with their returns, making Argentina a much less attractive investment destination.

Following the change in the exchange rate regime, the dollar will now move to a free-floating system between moving bands, abandoning the current 1% crawling peg that set the pace of depreciation of the peso against the U.S. dollar. From now on, the market itself will determine the equilibrium exchange rate through the mechanism of supply and demand for dollars by economic agents, while the dollar exchange rate will be unified, providing greater transparency to the market.

In my last article on Banco Galicia, I mentioned the possibility of this agreement being signed in April and addressed the opportunities that the lifting of the exchange rate restrictions would open up for Argentina with the influx of foreign capital into projects like Vaca Muerta and the country's real estate sector.

This new milestone in President Javier Milei's administration confirms the signal the markets had been hoping for and something many companies were expecting to send a new wave of investments to the country.

In words of Minister Caputo himself:

Ending the exchange rate controls within this macroeconomic context of fiscal and monetary policy will inevitably lead to the influx of investments that are currently pending until Argentina begins this new phase by ending these exchange rate restrictions. These investments will generate greater growth, which in turn will generate a larger surplus that will favor further tax cuts, more jobs, and higher wages.

After reorganizing public accounts based on a sharp 30% cut in public spending since the start of its administration, the Argentine government finally managed to eliminate a chronic fiscal deficit that the country had been carrying for decades, which was key to regaining the trust of the IMF technical staff. This was also achieved with economic growth, a recovery in wages, and a sharp drop in consumer prices for goods and services, which by the end of 2023 were heading towards hyperinflation.

It's also worth mentioning that one of the main drawbacks of the new measures announced by the Argentine government is some limitations on the withdrawal of dividends from previous years by foreign investors. Although companies will be able to freely buy and sell dollars from next week, they will be able to transfer dividends to their shareholders abroad only for those earnings accrued from 2025 onward. Meanwhile, for earnings prior to this year, companies will be able to choose to use their pesos to purchase a special bond issued by the Central Bank (Bopreal) that pays dollars at maturity. After that, these funds will be freely available to be sent abroad as dividends.

Mattress of Dollars

However, Argentina's macroeconomic stabilization program is not limited to the IMF and will have additional international support. In addition to the $12 billion of immediate free availability under the agreement with the IMF, Argentina is in advanced negotiations to receive another $6.1 billion from other international organizations and banks, in addition to a new repo line of credit for up to $2 billion and the renewal of the swap contract with China for up to $5 billion.

Thus, the Argentine president stated that by May, the BCRA's gross reserves are expected to be around $50 billion, of which approximately $28 billion will be available this year. This does not yet include the influx of dollars the country will receive as a result of the harvest season and the arrival of new investments, of which approximately $12 billion is already in projects covered by the new tax incentive regime (RIGI), mainly in the energy and mining sectors.

It's worth adding that this is happening amid a fiscal surplus and having effectively eliminated the unbacked issuance of pesos that was generating inflation. The Argentine central bank now has a strengthened financial and liquidity position to prevent a depreciation of the peso, conditions that didn't exist under previous programs. In fact, the BCRA itself could currently withdraw the country's entire monetary base in pesos (~AR$48T) in exchange for dollars, completely dollarizing the economy with freely available reserves.

The IMF staff projects annualized GDP growth of 5.5% for 2025 (raising its previous forecast of 5%) after a decade of economic stagnation during which the country experienced anemic levels of investment, causing factor productivity to collapse. The organization now forecasts very positive growth for the Argentine economy, driven by solid domestic demand and a carryover effect from stronger growth recorded during the second half of 2024. It also notes that growth in the medium term is expected to converge to around 3% as the stability and pro-market reforms implemented today bear fruit.

Exposure to Argentine Equity

With no growth catalysts in sight to revitalize the upward trend and after being the best ETF for the country globally in 2024, the ARGT has fallen 4% so far this year, the result of profit-taking after a meteoric rise last year and dragged down by the uncertainty prevailing in the markets during the first months of this year. However, despite the recent extreme volatility, the country's macro fundamentals remain very solid, to which is now added a potential catalyst for Argentine stocks.

ARGT has a fairly high exposure to MercadoLibre (NASDAQ:MELI), strongly correlated with the U.S. consumer discretionary sector, which has recently been severely punished by investors amid U.S.-international trade tensions. Furthermore, excluding MELI, we see significant participation in energy and financial companies, which I consider more attractive in this context. Particularly, those with exposure to the Vaca Muerta deposits and projects under development in that region of the country have the best medium- and long-term prospects. For their part, I believe Argentine banks will be among those benefiting from the arrival of investment to the country, much of which will be channeled through the local financial system.

That said, I consider ARGT a good option for gaining general exposure to the Argentine market without being overly selective, also boasting acceptable liquidity and nearly $1 billion in assets under management. On Friday, following the news of the government's lifting of foreign exchange controls, ARGT rose 5.91% after hours, closing an extremely volatile week with a strong 14% rally. However, the ETF's shares are still significantly lagging its regional peers so far this year.

Risks

The possibility that the new monetary regime could trigger a strong depreciation of the peso against the dollar early in the process represents one of the main risks, affecting the inflation levels recorded in recent months. March inflation rose 3.7% month-on-month, above the reading from previous months and an expected 2.6%, driven primarily by an increase in education and food prices. Confirmation of the upward reversal in the price index could once again generate distrust among agents regarding the feasibility of the government's monetary policy objectives and slow macroeconomic progress just months before the country's legislative elections.

Regarding the international scenario, Trump's measures could also cloud the outlook, as the intensification of trade tensions could affect global demand for commodities, negatively impacting Argentina's growth and its ability to access international capital markets. However, it should be noted that Argentine exports to the U.S. were only $6.5 billion (about 1% of GDP) in 2024, so the direct impact of the 10% tariff imposed by Washington on almost all countries in the world, including Argentina, would be less than 0.1% of GDP.

Conclusion

Despite a recent spike in inflation, the macroeconomic context remains highly favorable to Argentina. The agreement reached with the IMF, enabling the arrival of funds to strengthen the Central Bank's reserves, unlocks a wealth of investments that were pending a resolution. At the same time, the Milei administration demonstrates an unwavering commitment to carrying out its structural reform plan to restore the country's growth and restore the economic well-being it once enjoyed.

However, this does not come without internal and external challenges, which must be taken into account when investing in the Argentine market as a whole. In particular, the volatility in global markets resulting from a trade and tariff war between the United States and China has put stocks in jeopardy, especially Argentine ADRs listed on Wall Street, which have experienced sharp declines in recent weeks.

While I consider the country's fundamentals to be extraordinary, this latter aspect leads me to give ARGT a Hold rating while we wait for trade tensions to dissipate and for greater clarity regarding the trade policies of the warring powers.

data.title: Prologis: Business May Benefit From Trade War
data.description:
Financial markets are in a bad mood right now and are concerned about the trade war. But there are so many different companies trading in the U.S. market. And so, even in a trade war, there will be those companies that have more to gain than to lose. One such company I consider to be Prologis, Inc. (NYSE:PLD)

Company Portrait

Prologis excerpted from Wikipedia:

Prologis, Inc. is a real estate investment trust headquartered in San Francisco, California that invests in logistics facilities.[5] The company was formed through the merger of AMB Property Corporation and Prologis in June 2011, which made Prologis the largest industrial real estate company in the world.

The company has a huge amount of land and real estate owned. In the 2024 annual report, the following map of the company's total holdings was presented.

Prologis is the largest industrial lessor in the world. The company describes its activities as follows:

We predominately invest in logistics facilities. Our properties are typically used for distribution, storage, packaging, assembly, and light manufacturing of consumer products. The vast majority of our operating properties are used by our customers for retail and online fulfillment and business-to-business transactions.

In 2024, the company had revenue of $8.2 billion and profit of $3.7 billion. You can see more detailed figures in the table below.

With a price per share of $96, the company has a capitalization of $89 billion. The P/E ratio is 24 units. The company also has preferred stock (OTCQB:PLDGP) with a capitalization of $41 billion.

But the earnings numbers for Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT), which is what Prologis is, are not quite fair. That's because earnings take into account depreciation and completed transactions to buy or sell real estate. But it's not quite right to apply depreciation to real estate in assessing value, so there's a metric called FFO (Funds From Operations), which more accurately reflects a company's performance and cash flow. And the company's FFO in 2024 was $5.56 per share, while earnings were $4.01 per share. And FFO in 2024 compared to 2023 was down 0.9%, while earnings were up 21%. The company believes that in 2025, FFO will be $5.65 to $5.81 per share.

In the structure, Real Estate generates more than 90% of revenue. The weighted average lease term of customer contracts is 64 months. And long-term lease contracts guarantee stable cash flow performance of the company in the future.

Less than 10% of revenue is generated by the second line of business. Strategic Capital Segment includes revenues from asset management and property management services, transactional services for acquisition, disposition and leasing activity and promote revenue earned from the unconsolidated co-investment ventures.

The company has thousands of clients worldwide. Amazon (AMZN), The Home Depot (HD), FedEx (FDX), United Parcel Service (UPS), Geodis, NFI Industries, DHL, Walmart (WMT), lululemon athletica (LULU), and GigaCloud are in the top ten in terms of volume. Most of the business is in the U.S., as you can see from the table below, which shows the geography of revenues.

But here it should be said that the company often owns real estate in a share with other investors, and at the same time manages the assets of these investors, which, in fact, is the basis for the second business segment of the company, Strategic Capital Segment. Therefore, the total value of all real estate is much larger than the value of the company's assets.

Development Dynamics And Problems

In general, the company's business is clear: the company constructs buildings for industrial facilities and warehouses. Then, it either sells these facilities or sells part of the facility and further leases them out. It is a management company for investors who have also invested their funds in various properties of the company.

Of the recent trends, the company is actively building data centers. But the company's problem is that in the last year, with the exception of AI investment activity in the rest of the economy, the pace of building has been declining.

From the table below, you can see that the company had 55 construction starts in 2023, but in 2024, that number dropped to 26.

Although rental rates increased from 2021 and increased the company's rental income, this growth stopped in 2024. Plus, the U.S. had high inflation, and Fed Funds rates also increased from 2022. The high rates led to a cooling in the U.S. real estate market as a whole. It is also worth noting that yields on U.S. Treasury bonds and other instruments rose, which created a competitive alternative for real estate investments.

This affected the fact that the appetite for investing in real estate has decreased. And after a wave of rising demand for warehouses, after a pandemic in previous years, there is a cooling of the market here in 2024 as well. Because of this, the company appears to have seen a drop in new construction in 2024. Because of that, the company saw a drop in Strategic capital segment revenues.

These factors, I believe, have caused the company's stock to perform poorly over the past year despite the growth in total revenues and profits.

And of course, this year, the company's stock has fallen in value due to the general situation in the markets caused by the trade war.

Outlook

On April 16, the company will release its Q1 2025 earnings report. I don't expect any big changes, big positive or negative, compared to the stock's movement over the last month because the impact of the duty hike hasn't happened yet. Against the backdrop of the trade war, what happened in the first quarter is already in past economic conditions, so the first quarter report is already outdated, as well as all the past financial models and calculations. But most likely, if we look at the situation without the trade war, the report will probably show a continuation of the cooling trend in the industrial real estate market.

However, for investors, it is important to understand what conditions a company might find itself in during a trade war!!!!

Trump may have his own grandiose plans related to geopolitics, as I wrote about in one of my articles, but one way or another, the trade war has already started. A trade war can disrupt more familiar supply chains, which can lead to chaos where congestion in the movement of goods can occur. I think that many companies may continue to produce their goods, but the speed of processing them at customs will drop, and perhaps many may decide to wait to sell the goods in the hope that duties will be reduced. The congestion will lead to higher inventories and may overload transportation, warehousing, and logistics infrastructure.

This situation is to Prologis's advantage. What for other companies will be a problem, in the form of increased logistics costs, for Prologis will be an increase in the utilization of its warehouses. In the short term, that could give the company a very nice boost in revenue.

Trump and U.S. Industry

Donald Trump has repeatedly stated that he wants manufacturing to come back to the U.S., and that is the reason for raising duties. I don't know how feasible his wishes are, given that labor is expensive in the U.S. and not all manufacturing will benefit from moving their production to the U.S. Nevertheless, some portion of companies will eventually move their plants and factories to the U.S.

And Prologis builds real estate for industry. Moving manufacturing to the U.S. creates potential new customers for the company!!! If you take the production of some list of goods of children's toys, the production of which will be conditionally moved to the U.S. from China in the coming years. Such production is exactly the ideal client for Prologis. It would be expensive for a toy manufacturer to buy and build its own building because it is a capital-intensive investment. But to rent or build a building using the Prologis business model with equity participation will be just as profitable.

It is also worth understanding one more thing!!! To grow 1 kg of chicken, you need to spend 3 kg of feed. If you bring in finished chickens, you need to process 1 kg of loads, and if you raise chickens, you need to process 4 kg of loads. I think it works the same way in industry. Importing a good involves a one-time transportation of the good from point A to point B. Manufacturing a good may require multiple movements of raw materials and components at different stages of the production chain.

I think that if American industry increases its production by a notional $100 billion to replace Chinese imports, in terms of logistical burden, this could offset the overall drop in consumption by a multiple of that amount, as in the chicken example. In my opinion, the increase in production activity will lead to the fact that not only finished goods but also raw materials, components, and finished goods themselves will have to be transported and stored. This should increase the total load on warehouses and logistics. And this is again favorable for Prologis because it rents out warehouses. Therefore, what for other companies will be a cost for Prologis will be revenue.

It should be added that the potential easing of the Federal Reserve's monetary policy may have a positive impact on the availability of capital and may have a positive impact on the real estate market as a whole.

Risks

The main risk is that the overall downturn in the U.S. economy could be stronger than the effect I described above. But in this case, we would be talking about a full-blown prolonged economic crisis in the US, in which the entire market would fall very hard.

Logistics failures can only temporarily cause inventory to rise. In a prolonged crisis in the economy, turnover may eventually fall. Also, in a prolonged crisis, investment activity may be depressed, hindering growth in the industry.

However, I think an excessive downturn in the economy will still force Trump to hit the brakes, or the brakes may be hit by Congress with the Senate, or the Fed may turn on the printing press.

Summary

Based on the above, I can say that in the past reality, Prologis was not that interesting; it had a stable and reliable business with a strong balance sheet that was dependent on interest rates and activity in the US economy and was exposed to general cycles in the economy. But at the same time, the company did not have any prospects for strong growth beyond the general trends in the economy.

Now in a new environment where there are apparently going to be big changes. In the short term, the company may benefit from the mess in the logistics system and the resulting increase in inventories due to the resulting congestion in the movement of goods. In the long term, increased industrial activity in the U.S., even partly due to the imposition of duties, will increase demand for the company's building services and demand for warehousing, as the turnover of goods, raw materials, and components is likely to increase.

Company Shares

In this changed environment, the company's shares, which have fallen in price, at the level of about $90-100, are attractive for long-term investment. But, of course, it should be realized that in such a volatile market, it will be difficult for the company's shares to grow against the market. So I think it's only worth buying if you're investing for the long term, with the assumption that the company's shares may still fall after the rest of the market.

In terms of targets, given the volatility in the markets, I can't tell you where the bottom will be and when. I can only assume that in terms of numbers, what I described above will affect the company's performance in the second quarter at the earliest, and then perhaps my speculation will be confirmed, and the stock could turn upward.

As for upside targets, I think the stock could recover to the $125 a share price it was at earlier this year before the wave of declines in the markets, even if the market as a whole fails to recover. And under favorable circumstances, the stock could possibly exceed that price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Castle Biosciences: Steady Revenue Stream, But Medicare Reimbursement Loss A Concern
data.description:
Castle Biosciences (NASDAQ:CSTL) develops diagnostic tests for patients with skin, gastrointestinal, and mental health conditions. One of their first tests was DecisionDx-Melanoma used in early-stage skin cancer to predict lymph node involvement and recurrence risk. That test still makes up a big chunk of revenue, but Castle has since expanded into other areas with products like DecisionDx-SCC for high-risk squamous cell carcinoma, TissueCypher for Barrett’s esophagus, and IDgenetix for personalized psychiatric prescribing.

Castle’s melanoma test is well adopted in community dermatology practices, but not as much in academic centers due to lack of guideline inclusion. Despite that, it’s adding new clinicians every year and pushing for broader use within practices already using the test. The company is also dealing with a potential Medicare reimbursement loss for its SCC test, which could take a bite out of revenue starting in Q2 2025. As they state in the latest earnings call:

In January 2025, Novitas, the Medicare administrative contract with jurisdiction over our laboratory in Pittsburgh, finalized the local coverage determination, or LCD, that included the language signifying noncoverage by Medicare for DecisionDx-SCC.

Going deeper into the SCC test, the company tells us two things – a) their SCC tests help patients by its "ability to predict the risk of metastasis impacting treatment pathways and the ability to predict responsiveness to adjuvant radiation therapy," and b) by achieving "substantial savings to the Medicare program of up to $972 million per year," as determined by "a cost-effectiveness article that was published in January 2024." In discussing the main avenues left to the company, management gave us two approaches – one through Novitas directly and the other through another contract partner, MolDX. The former approach may involve a reconsideration request, or wait for the results of a lawsuit filed by another company against Novitas on this issue.

On the flip side, TissueCypher is picking up steam fast and getting recognized in GI guidelines. Castle is well capitalized, sitting on nearly $300 million in cash, and while it’s burning a bit of cash now, it has plenty of runway to invest in growth or pursue smart acquisitions.

Currently, the company offers a number of tests, all of which are marketed as Laboratory Developed Tests (LDTs), meaning they're not exactly FDA approved, but many have Medicare coverage. Castle Biosciences has a portfolio of molecular diagnostic tests in dermatology, gastroenterology, and mental health. Its flagship product, DecisionDx-Melanoma, helps assess the risk of recurrence and lymph node involvement in early-stage melanoma. DecisionDx-SCC is used for high-risk cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma to predict metastasis and response to adjuvant radiation therapy. TissueCypher evaluates the risk of progression from Barrett’s esophagus to esophageal cancer and is gaining traction following inclusion in GI practice guidelines. IDgenetix, Castle’s pharmacogenomic test, supports medication selection in psychiatric care, though it has recently been deprioritized due to weak uptake. The company has also signaled plans to launch a gene expression test for atopic dermatitis in late 2025, pending successful validation.

Revenue and Analysis

Below is a consolidated table showing their Medicare rates and annual revenues:

Castle Biosciences grew full-year revenue by 51% YoY to $332.1M in 2024, with test volume up 36%, indicating both increased adoption and favorable reimbursement dynamics. However, growth is not uniform across the portfolio.

As we can see from the table above, DecisionDx-Melanoma is a mature product with a stable reimbursement rate throughout. It has shown steady growth, from $16.2M in 2020 to $36.0M in 2024—a 122% increase. However, growth rate has been modest recently, only 8% YoY in 2024. That suggests that it's entering a mature adoption phase, despite a sizable remaining addressable market (28% penetration of ~130K eligible patients). Castle is still adding ~1,800 new ordering clinicians annually, consistent across 2023 and 2024. This suggests meaningful greenfield opportunity remains, especially in community practices, new entrants (residents, PAs), and late adopters. While some clinicians are already using the test across all eligible patient types, others are limiting use to specific Breslow thicknesses or “sweet spots.” Castle is now focusing on expanding use within existing accounts by identifying underused patient segments and providing targeted education, which could drive continued volume growth despite modest YoY headline figures (+8% in 2024).

In dermatology, DecisionDx-SCC is the fastest growing test, with revenue growing from only $0.5M in 2020 to $16.3M in 2024 – a 32x increase. In 2023, it received ADLT designation, which helped them more than double the rate. Its TAM is ~200K high-risk cases/year, so it's likely still in a market penetration phase and will improve revenues over the years. DecisionDx-SCC delivered 43% volume growth YoY, but faces critical downside risk in 2025 due to the pending non-coverage LCD from Novitas, potentially eliminating Medicare reimbursement from April 2025 onward. This is a material risk to one of CSTL’s highest-growth assets.

The company’s Diagnostic GEP Offering (MyPath + DiffDx) has had a flat performance, with ~$3.9M annually since 2022. It has a low Medicare rate, and there's internal consolidation, with only MyPath being offered post-2023, which may limit future growth.

The entire dermatology test segment has seen a 3.3x increase in these five years. While melanoma is still the highest revenue generator, SCC is driving growth and revenue momentum.

The company has been steadily expanding their non-dermatologic offerings, with a major breakout, for example, in TissueCypher revenues, which jumped 131% YoY from $9.1M → $21.0M in 2024. This could be the next revenue pillar, although we don’t have Medicare data. TissueCypher’s favorable inclusion in AGA guidelines and early market traction (5% penetration of 415K U.S. patients) support this trajectory.

A similar breakout is also seen with IDgenetix, which jumped from $3.2M (2022) → $17.2M (2024). This test offers personalized psychotropic prescribing, broadening the company’s offerings into a vastly different – and large – segment. Again, we don’t have Medicare data, although the high revenues hint at high adoption rate success. However, note that in its earnings call, the company says:

Turning to our mental health business, due to changes in the market and our focus on allocating resources efficiently on profitable growth, in late 2024, we revised our commercial strategy for IDgenetix tests, reallocating resources to inside sales and nonpersonal promotions. In December 2024, we observed month-to-month decreases in IDgenetix test reports, which persisted throughout year-end 2024. We continue to offer our IDgenetix test and monitor performance. However, in 2025, we expect test report volumes and net revenues will continue to decrease and the long-term performance of this test remains uncertain.

Lastly, we have uveal melanoma, which is a niche market, as reflected in the long-term flat revenue of DecisionDx-UM. Although it has a high Medicare rate, it's unlikely to contribute much to topline growth.

Thus, what we see here is that Medicare pricing matters, with tests with higher rates becoming high earners. However, at the same time, volume growth is also becoming important.

The other important takeaway is that diversification is working, with non-derm products making up to ~41% of test revenue. Also note that while revenue growth shows some traction, long-term sustainability will depend on payer coverage, guideline inclusion, and competitive positioning. Also, volume is important because strategizing on reimbursement rate hike-based growth may not be sustainable.

Financials

CSTL has a market cap of $561mn and a cash balance of $293mn. R&D and G&A expenses were $11.8mn and $50mn respectively. Their CFO was $24.4mn while capex was ($7.6mn), giving them a positive FCF of $16.8mn, which helps with the runway.

Risks

Castle Biosciences makes most of its money from just a few diagnostic tests, and a big chunk of that comes from Medicare. That’s fine when everything is working, but it also means the company is exposed if Medicare ever changes how it pays for those tests – like it may do with SCC. Their two main products—DecisionDx-Melanoma and DecisionDx-SCC—are priced fairly high, and if Medicare lowers those rates or changes its coverage rules, it could hit revenue hard. Castle has added newer tests like TissueCypher and IDgenetix, which are showing growth, but they’re still early-stage and not yet proven to be long-term revenue drivers. IDgenetix, specifically, is being sidelined. It’s also not guaranteed that doctors will keep using these newer tests without more clinical uptake and routine adoption.

On the financial side, Castle has a big cash cushion—$293 million—which gives it over four years of breathing room at its current spending rate. But it’s still burning cash each quarter, and most of its spending goes to general and admin costs, not research. That could be a problem if revenue doesn’t keep up. They’ve also bought several companies recently, expanding beyond skin cancer into GI and mental health testing. While that helps diversify the business, it also means they now have to manage different specialties and selling strategies at once, which isn’t easy. With low Wall Street coverage and a small market cap, the stock can be overlooked or volatile, even when the core business is moving in the right direction.

Bottom Line

Trading near 52-week lows, CSTL stock now offers a good entry point for people looking to buy into a company with an unexciting but steady business model and a good revenue stream. On the other hand, I think people may want to hold off until there is more clarity on the SCC non-coverage issue.

data.title: Apple: Minimal Impact From Upcoming Tariffs On Semiconductors
data.description:
Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) has had some of the most dramatic moves, both up and down, among the Magnificent 7 this past week. The chart below shows the performance of the Mag 7, with Apple represented by the dark blue line.

Honestly, if someone told me this chart was a bunch of athletes' heart monitors during a 100-meter sprint layered together, I'd probably believe it.

Naturally, the 145% tariffs on imports from China caused the market to panic, triggering a 24% drop in Apple's shares between April 3 and 8, wiping out $800 billion of its market cap. In fact, Apple briefly lost its title as the world's most valuable publicly listed company to Microsoft (MSFT) last week, just to reclaim it back today.

Apple's selloff was so pronounced due to its heavy reliance on manufacturing in China. According to some analysts, about 90% of iPhones, over half of Macs, and up to 80% of iPads are made there.

Last Friday, U.S. Customs released new guidance on reciprocal tariffs, stating that semiconductors and electronic devices are exempt from the reciprocal tariffs. In other words, Apple apparently is exempt from the 145% tariff on Chinese imports.

That report sparked a frenzy on Monday, sending Apple’s share price up by more than 7% during the intraday session. However, the party didn't last too long, as Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said on Sunday that new, sector-specific tariffs targeting semiconductors and related electronics are expected within one to two months. Later that day, Trump confirmed the news in a post, adding that these products (semis and electronic devices) are already subject to the 20% Fentanyl tariffs.

As you are about to find out, those few bolded words above are a key part of my bull case for Apple.

Let's zoom out first and review the bigger picture before I go into the details of my thesis.

The Big Picture

At this point, I have a high conviction that the worst of the tariff drama is behind us now, despite the uncertainty (at the moment of writing this article) around sector wide tariffs on semiconductors. My rationale (emphasis on "my," as this is my own opinion) is extremely simple.

I believe the pause on tariffs, which, as you remember, was first rumored and then denied by the White House just two days before it was officially announced on April 9, was most likely motivated by the market's reaction to the new trade policy. Here is one of Trump's replies when asked why he had ordered the pause:

People were jumping a little bit out of line. They were getting yippy.

I certainly believe that the new administration is carefully watching the broader market's reaction to their tariff agenda, particularly after the 10% selloff in the S&P 500 over April 3 and 4.

I don't think the new administration wants to see another panic selloff following the announcement/amendment of their trade policies. Therefore, I expect the tariffs on semiconductors to be softer than what the market fears.

In fact, I believe that there will be almost no meaningful impact for Apple (or any other company importing from China) on these tariffs. Why?

First, in 2024, smartphones were the top product the U.S. imported from China, totaling $41.7 billion. Laptops made in China came in second at $33.1 billion.

Second, take a look at the following excerpt from Trump's latest post on this topic in Truth Social (emphasis added):

There was no Tariff “exception” announced on Friday. These products are subject to the existing 20% Fentanyl Tariffs, and they are just moving to a different Tariff “bucket.”

In other words, electronic devices made in China are already affected by a 20% tariff. This is key!

As Trump said in his post, these products (semis, smartphones, laptops, etc.) are just moving to a different tariff category. Based on Trump's wording from above, I don’t expect the tariffs on semiconductors to stack up on top of the 20% Fentanyl tariffs.

Back in January, Trump hinted at a range between 25% and 100% for tariffs on semiconductors. Assuming 25% tariffs on semis (again, I believe the administration is monitoring the broader markets; therefore, any value higher than 25% will most likely tank the markets), the real impact for Apple will be just 5 additional percentage points to the already 20% tariff they are paying.

Let me be even more clear. I project that the new tariffs on semis (possibly 25%) will supersede the current 20% Fentanyl tariff. With these tariffs being the last spike in the market's toe, I expect the Mag 7 to rally over the next 80 days, once these are announced this week.

As to what will happen following the pause on reciprocal tariffs, I admit I have no clue. I'd even argue that the administration isn't sure either, especially with the recent hint at a pause on auto tariffs.

Zooming In

Over the past 12 months, iPhone sales accounted for 50% of Apple's total revenue.

That's $200.6 billion in iPhone sales (TTM). Unfortunately, Apple doesn't disclose in its financial reporting the exact number of smartphones manufactured in China that are later imported into the U.S. External reports suggest this figure may be close to 90%.

Let's do a rough estimate on the impact that a potential 25% tariff would have on Apple's most sold iPhone model in Q4 last year, the iPhone 16, which accounted for 68% of all iPhone sales, according to CIRP estimates.

According to analysts from TechInsights, the bill of materials for an iPhone 16 Pro, preassembly, is about $550. I considered including below a visual from The Wall Street Journal that perfectly resembles the cost per component.

After accounting for (cheap) assembly and testing costs, the factory cost of an iPhone 16 Pro is approximately $580. At the moment of writing this article, this model is sold in the U.S. for $1,100.

Before any tariffs, the estimated gross margin for this model is a respectable 47%, or $520.

Considering the 20% tariff on Fentanyl, the margin is still positive, sitting at 36.7%.

Assuming a non-cumulative 25% tariff on semis and electronics, the estimated gross margin would be 34%. That's a 13 percentage point deterioration from the no tariff scenario.

Here, I'm assuming that Apple absorbs 100% of the extra costs ($145) due to the tariff. If we split the cost between Apple and consumers, the final price of the iPhone 16 Pro would be around $73 more, leading to a margin of 44%. That would be a 3 percentage point deterioration in gross margins. Considering the profitability margins below, I don't think a 3 pps headwind in gross margins will lead to a significant impact.

Additionally, a possible price hike of $73 is very manageable considering the strong moat of the company and "fan" base. Therefore, I believe that demand (and therefore, sales) will be minimally impacted by a possible 25% tariff on semis.

Risks

My bull case is leaning on two main pillars.

  • First, I believe the new tariff on semiconductors and electronic devices imported from China will be set at 25%.
  • Second, I'm assuming the new tariff will not be added on top of the existing 20% Fentanyl tariff.

Let's assume a worst-case scenario where the new tariff on semiconductors and electronic devices is 100% (the upper range of Trump's range back in January) and the 20% tariff on Fentanyl goes on top of that, essentially, bringing the effective tariff to 120%.

In this case, the factory cost of an iPhone 16 Pro would be $1,275. That’s higher than the current retail price of $1,100, which means a negative margin of 16 percent.

Assuming Apple absorbs 50% of the tariff costs, consumers would have to pay $350 more, which brings the total cost of the device to $1,450, with a gross margin of 35.8%. I believe this figure is enough to have a considerable impact on demand and on Apple's bottom line.

As I said before, the uncertainty surrounding these tariffs is so high that I wouldn't completely write off this scenario.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I expect the new tariffs on semiconductors and electronic devices (smartphones, tablets, laptops, wearables, etc.) to land around 25%. Given that these devices are already subject to the 20% Fentanyl tariff, a possible 5 percentage point increase will not be enough to deteriorate demand for Apple's products, considering the strong moat of the company. As demonstrated above, the impact of these tariffs on the iPhone 16 Pro's gross margins would be manageable.

Major risks include the uncertain trade policy, with several tariffs paused for weeks and, in some cases, even days after they were announced. If the new tariffs on semiconductors are not in my expected range of 25% or if they aggregate on top of the 20% Fentanyl tariff, my bull case could fall apart.

I think there's a low chance this event will happen, as I'm confident the new administration is keeping a close eye on how these tariffs are affecting the broader market. I expect a rally after the sector wide semiconductor tariff is announced. I rate Apple as a strong buy.

data.title: Trump's Tariffs Contain An Important Lesson For Investors
data.description:
Somewhat reluctantly, I'm going to say a few words about Mr Trump and his tariffs.

I say "reluctantly", because long-term investors should be focused on the long term, and that means not worrying too much about what happens, economically or geopolitically, on a daily, weekly or even monthly basis. In the long run, these things rarely have any meaningful impact, and when there is a meaningful impact, it's almost impossible to predict how they will affect markets over the next few years.

The 2009 global financial crisis is a perfect example. At the time, it felt like the end of the financial system and perhaps the end of modern civilisation. We were on the brink of collapse, preparing baked beans and shotguns, and yet two years later the FTSE 100 was up more than 50%.

With hindsight (and perhaps even without it), panic-selling in early 2009 was a terrible idea, and I think that's likely to be true for those who panic-sold when Trump announced his tariffs.

That's because panic selling is almost never the right answer.

Instead, this chaotic episode is yet another reminder that in an uncertain world, diversification is almost always king.

Diversification means diversifying your investments across many companies, many industries and many countries, because no company, industry or country is guaranteed to thrive or even survive forever. They all go through cycles, from innovation and growth to maturity and stability, and then perhaps to decline, as newer, more innovative and harder working companies, industries and countries take the lead.

The principle of diversification should also be applied when looking within individual companies.

For example, many moons ago, I invested in a tiny UK company that made DVD cases and it sold about 80% of its output to one supermarket chain. When DVD's started to become obsolete about 15 years ago, the supermarket chain decided not to renew its purchase contract and the DVD case-maker was unable to replace the near-instantaneous loss of 80% of its sales. And that, as they say, was the end of that.

So when you're analysing a potential investment, look for single points of failure that could bring the company to its knees, or at the very least, cause it material and long-lasting harm.

As I mentioned in the previous example, this often shows up as a single large customer, which I define as any customer generating more than 10% of a company's total revenue. Another example would be a single supplier (excluding electricity, gas and other utility suppliers), or perhaps a single key manufacturing site or distribution hub. Buildings do burn down, and even with insurance this can be extremely disruptive.

As Trump has so successfully demonstrated, it's also important to avoid excessive exposure to a single country, because no country is guaranteed a magic carpet ride to eternal riches. History is littered with the remnants of once-great empires, so if you're looking for multi-decadal stability, geographic diversity is essential.

In my case, about 40% of my portfolio's revenues are generated in what remains of a once-mighty empire (the UK), and perhaps 33% would be a more sensible number (i.e. twice as much revenue generated outside any one country than within it).

With broad diversification as the foundation, you can then select companies with other defensive attributes, like consistently strong profitability, strong balance sheets and multiple decades of unbroken dividend payments. Put all of that together, and you should have a portfolio that is likely to generate satisfactory levels of income and growth over the medium and longer term, across a wide range of highly uncertain futures.

And as an added bonus, your stress levels and your desire to panic-sell during the next inevitable crisis will likely be lower, too.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Value Line: Fairly Priced For Now
data.description:
Investment thesis

Shares of Value Line (NASDAQ:VALU) are down nearly 25% since my previous writeup where I stated that the stock was a Hold, citing its high valuation. While the share price decline has brought its valuation multiple down considerably, I argue that the current market environment where the major US indexes have seen significant declines so far this year, will put pressure on the company's asset management business going forward. The declining revenue and margins in its publishing business remains a major concern, which together with other risks I see, make VALU stock unappealing at the current valuation. My estimate for fair value is $39.40 per share, and I am therefore maintaining my Hold rating on the stock.

Decline in publishing revenue persists while asset management shines

As shown above, Q3 results for fiscal '25 showed that total publishing revenue was down 1.8% year over year. While the declines were slightly lower than those seen in the prior two quarters, income from publishing was down more than 30%. The lower margins were due to higher operating expenses, including higher advertising costs. This is indeed concerning, as the trend of lower revenue and margins is set to continue, as I see no clear path for this trend to reverse.

On the other hand, Value Line's asset management business EULAV, demonstrated impressive results, with overall income growing nearly 41% year over year, as shown above. This was likely driven by growth in assets under management, benefitting from the overall US stock market performing well towards the end of last year. Notably, asset management contribution flows directly to Value Line's bottom line after deductions for tax payments.

Business outlook and VALU stock valuation

Value Line's publishing business is set to bring in close to $7 million in net income for fiscal 2025. Given the declining revenues expected going forward, coupled with margins coming under pressure, I am inclined to adopt a conservative approach to valuing this business. A relevant peer like MarketWise (MKTW) which I covered recently with a Buy rating, has also struggled with generating organic growth and currently trades at only a high single digit FCF multiple. At the other end of the spectrum, a peer like Morningstar (MORN) which is demonstrating high single digit growth rates, is valued at over 30 times earnings. On the whole, I believe that a earnings multiple of 10 is justified for Value Line's publishing business, which puts its valuation at $70 million.

While some investors may argue that the publishing business deserves a lower valuation, I will point out that the key driver for VALU stock comes from its asset management segment. Net income from this segment is set to exceed $15 million for fiscal '25. However, these earnings can be quite volatile, and can fluctuate broadly in line with the stock market. As shown above, earnings fell sharply in 2023 due to weaker stock market performance in the prior year, which led to a decline in assets under management. Judging by this, the weaker stock market performance so far this year is likely to be a major headwind to earnings in the coming quarters.

Given the more volatile nature of these earnings, I believe the valuation multiple applied to it should represent a significant discount compared to private asset managers growing at similar rates such as StepStone Group (STEP) and Hamilton Lane (HLNE). These peers tend to trade at earnings multiples that are over 25. Meanwhile Artisan Partners Asset Management (APAM) which mainly invests in public equities and is growing at mid single digit rates, is valued at close to 11 times earnings. Taking this into consideration, if we assign a multiple of 15 to asset management earnings, this would put the valuation for this segment at $225 million. Notably, this is more than three times the value of the publishing business.

Now if we add the valuation of the two business units to the company's cash and short term investments of $76 million, we end up with a total value of $371 million. This translates to a share price of $39.40, based on 9.4 million shares outstanding. This implies that there is hardly any upside left from the current share price. I acknowledge that strong performance ahead in public market equities could significantly boost earnings and provide significant upside. On the contrary I also see meaningful risks that could negatively impact the current outlook, which I will walk through next.

Risks

Continued macroeconomic weakness could result in the overall stock market declining further, which would be a headwind to growth in assets under management. Furthermore, while the declining publishing revenue and margins is a concern, a bigger risk for investors is if the Value Line brand gradually loses its appeal due to this. Additionally, I would point that if we look at the performance of several of its funds such as the Value Line Select Growth Fund (VALSX) and Value Line Mid Cap Focused Fund (VLIFX), they have significantly underperformed the S&P 500 Index (SPX) over the past five years.

Other risks of note include the high revenue concentration in its publishing segment, with its largest customer accounting for 32.2% of total publishing revenues in fiscal 2024. It has also been disappointing to see capital returns to shareholders remaining muted, with the current dividend yield at just over 3%, with barely any share purchases done over the past few quarters. The lack of earnings calls and Wall Street coverage on the stock, make it challenging to understand management's vision and targets for the company.

Conclusion

While future investor returns hinge primarily on growth in its asset management business, the outlook for it does not look favorable given the ongoing market conditions. The current valuation for the stock remains unappealing considering the risks that I see. I am therefore sticking to the sidelines for now.

data.title: LexinFintech: My Thoughts On The Recent Sell-Off And Relative Value; Sell Recommendation
data.description:
Thesis

My recommendation for LexinFintech Holdings Ltd. (NASDAQ:LX) is a "sell". Amid the recent market downturn, the impact on LX has been even more pronounced. This has to do with several factors including the stock's reliance on market momentum in generating returns, high beta, and its small market capitalization of only $1.2 billion. If we assess the appeal of LX relative to its Chinese fintech peers, its valuation is difficult to justify. In today's environment with limited risk appetite, LX is perhaps not the best idea. If in case, you are a long-term investor with a high appetite for short-term volatility, I would be more inclined to invest in its competitors.

Company Background

LexinFintech is a fintech company based in China. It uses proprietary technology to help banks assess the credit risk of consumer borrowers, originate loans for banks, and service the loans. From 2020 to 2024, origination volumes per annum ranged from 177 billion CNY to 250 billion CNY (see the company's 4th quarter filings and CNY/USD = c7.3).

The company was founded in 2013 and went public in 2018. In 2018, total assets were around 12 billion CNY compared to 22 billion in 2024. Growth is pretty much in line with peers given the financial services industry's sensitivity to the overall macro environment and the similarity in the operating models of these fintech companies. Two close competitors are FinVolution Group (FINV) and Qifu Technology, Inc. (QFIN). Both have been exhibiting strong fundamentals, which we will dig into shortly.

Chart: LX exhibits strong 1 year momentum

Performance in the recent sell-off and technicals

As a small-cap (~$1bn) and high beta (c1.79) stock, LX had suffered a lot since the market turmoil in the aftermath of Trump's liberation day. Since the 8th of April, the S&P had recovered mildly from the slump as the VIX and term-premium implied recession probability came down. However, LX failed to follow. This could indicate a continued risk-off sentiment for stocks of its characteristics or a potential mis-pricing opportunity. If we look closer, the brief rebound in the market after Trump's 90-day tariff pause has highlighted returns in more aggressive factors such as momentum and growth. Both are quite relevant for LX, especially momentum with its 273% 1-year price performance. So, the decline for LX is likely not due to systematic factors.

You might ask, since this retaliation puts China in the spotlight, does the underperformance for LX or any of its peers for that matter have to do with China's risk? Mostly no, as we've seen a robust recovery for China ETFs such as FXI, CHIQ, and the overall Chinese equities index, CSI300. Therefore, the persistent and intense decline of LX and its peers is a bit of a conundrum.

The risk factors (e.g., momentum, small-cap) don't really support the underperformance of LX and its entire peers, it isn't completely about the showdown between the US and China, then what can it be about? Here are two factors that I'd like to discuss:

1. Market efficiency - An overall rebound is difficult to justify: As the geopolitical pressure mounts between the US and China, it is quite unexpected for me to see the bullish performance of Chinese equities during the same period. It could be due to the fact that previous sell-offs were driven by market risk premiums instead of fundamental expectations of Chinese companies.

The market went up on the 8th of April, followed by a minor downturn the next day. Meanwhile, long-term China treasury yields remain suppressed while the spike in long-term US treasury yields could be due to a technical sell-off instead of a reflection of the market expectation of growth. The persistent rise in corporate IG and HY bond yields also voiced concerns on fundamentals. Overall, there are still ample risks ahead. So, even if LX did not follow the brief market rebound, it doesn't mean that it should.

2. Technicals: What stands out for LX is its 1.79 beta value (24-month), 4.45% short interest, and a relatively high 3-month volume to its shares outstanding (~2.5%). All of these make the stock relatively vulnerable to a downturn and more volatile, especially in an environment of limited clarity. Nonetheless, the downward pressure of the stock due to the mentioned aspect will likely be temporary with a 60-month beta level of 0.36. But for now, those technical concerns are important risks to highlight.

Reflecting on those conditions, it doesn't make me much positive on the stock and to think that there will be short-term upsides. There is still much uncertainty in general and for the stock individually. These reasons alone already form a large part of my bearish thesis on the stock.

Fundamental and peer analysis

In the aftermath of the market turmoil since April, valuations for LX and its peers started to converge. However, LexinFintech is still relatively more expensive which, I think, is not justified. Here are my reasons:

1. Growth expectations: Forward p/e ratio for LX points to potential value. However, that is misguided due to excessive earnings expectations while revenue growth expectations are in line with peers.

2. Asset quality lags peers: In comparison with two fintech peers FinVolution Group (FINV) and Qifu Technology, Inc. (QFIN), LexinFintech has generally shown a higher 90+ days delinquency ratio. If the high earnings growth expectation is based on the compression of credit costs to an extent that is much stronger than competitors, it is unlikely.

3. Higher cost of leverage: Lastly, LexinFintech faces a much higher net debt in its capital structure, which further limits earnings growth. The limitation on earnings also has to do with its relatively lower profitability.

Relevant metrics summarizing the above points

Conclusion

My bearish thesis on LexinFintech is formed by three legs: 1) market risks are quite pronounced considering geopolitics and macro fundamentals moving forward 2) the technical features of the stock and its sensitivity to volatility make it an unfavourable bet 3) relative valuation is weak. On the other hand, I am overall bullish on Chinese banking and fintech considering China's easing credit cycle and the sector's offer on value. To that end, I'd recommend a look at its peers and my recent article on FinVolution.

data.title: Dow Jones, S&P 500 Forecast: Tech Tariff Pause Boosts Sentiment But Risks Remain
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Technology stocks surged, boosting Asian and European shares on Monday, after President Trump excluded smartphones and computers from China tariffs. This gave markets a breather after a week of sharp ups and downs.

Wall Street Indexes look set to follow suit, with gains expected for major tech giants. There was still uncertainty as President Trump said on Sunday that tariffs on semiconductors would be announced within a week, and a decision on phones would come "soon."

Having finished last week on a positive note, market participants will be hoping for further gains this week. There are however, a host of risks to consider and markets are by no means out of the woods just yet. Expect further volatility as the tariff picture continues to develop.

On Friday last week, the fear and greed index was around the 13 mark which leaves it in extreme fear territory despite the positive end to the week. This should not come as a surprise as the recent US equity market sell-off ranks among the most severe short-term declines since 1929.

Monday will be a quiet day in terms of data, but tariff announcements or updates may move the needle for US indices. There is also a host of Federal Reserve policymakers on the docket later in the day which could add further uncertainty for market participants to navigate.

Technical Analysis - Dow Jones and S&P 500

Dow Jones (US30) Daily Chart, April 14, 2025

From a technical standpoint, the Dow Jones finished last week with a gain of around 5.22% and gapped higher over the weekend.

The gap higher down to the developments over the weekend regarding technology equipment being exempt from tariffs, for the time being.

The 14-period RSI on a daily timeframe may be a good indicator to keep an eye on. The RSI is currently approaching the 50 neutral level with a rejection likely to lead to fresh lows and a sign that momentum remains in favor of bears. A break above the 50 mark may be seen as a sign of a shift in momentum and could embolden bulls.

Should bulls prevail, markets will be eyeing 41095 and 41400 before the 41950 handle comes into focus.

A bearish push from here may find support at the psychological 40000 mark before 39588 and 39000 come into focus.

Support

  • 40000
  • 39588
  • 39000

Resistance

  • 41095
  • 41400
  • 41950

S&P 500

Looking at the S&P 500 and the chart almost mirrors the Dow Jones.

I will also be paying close attention to the period-14 RSI on a daily timeframe to monitor any potential momentum shifts.

A break higher does face some crucial levels with the 5500 handle also hosting the 20-day MA before markets eye 5538 and 5635.

A push lower for the S&P 500 and markets will look for potential support at 5391, 5330 and 5267 respectively.

For both indexes tariff developments and overall sentiment hold the keys. Any moves will largely depend on how this develops over the course of the day and week ahead.

S&P 500 Daily Chart, April 14, 2025

Support

  • 5391
  • 5330
  • 5267

Resistance

  • 5500
  • 5538
  • 5635

Original Post

data.title: Robinhood: Cash-Rich And Ready To Run
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Robinhood Markets, Inc. (NASDAQ:HOOD) has a lot going for it. For one, the business is highly innovative, consistently launching new products to satisfy its rapidly growing customer base.

But what makes Robinhood particularly compelling is that it's highly successful at monetizing its user base to grow its revenue line.

What's more, the business generates a lot of free cash flow, which I estimate could reach around $1.5 billion of free cash flow in 2025.

For this debt-free business, paying around 26x forward free cash flow strikes me as a bargain.

Rapid Recap

Robinhood is a stock that I'd been bullish on and recommended to Deep Value Returns subscribers. Here's the date I recommended the stock and the date I sold the stock.

At the time that I recommended selling the stock HOOD was approximately $39 per share, nailing down a large gain in less than 1 year. And what now?

Robinhood's Near-Term Prospects

Robinhood makes it super easy for traders to trade stocks, options, and crypto.

What makes Robinhood stand out is how simple and user-friendly it is. There are no trading commissions, and they’ve packed in a bunch of tools that help trade a lot and quickly.

Furthermore, they’ve recently added a premium credit card as well as better tools for serious traders.

Basically, Robinhood wants to be the go-to financial app for the next generation, offering everything from investing and borrowing to crypto, as a super-app.

Keep in mind that Robinhood's Q4 2024 just saw their first-ever $1 billion revenue quarter, and they’re planning more growth by rolling out new features, including expanding their crypto offerings.

Altogether, they’ve got three significant product launches this year—one focused on their Gold membership, another on crypto, and a third for active traders.

All sounds great, right? Well, here's one part of the problem.

For the month of February, we saw Assets Under Custody shrink by 8% y/y. This means that as the market goes down, as you may expect, Assets Under Custody also go down.

And if we were to presume that markets remain volatile for a while, this could impact its ability to grow its assets under custody, which would impact its ability to grow its revenues. Given this context, let's now discuss its financials.

Revenue Growth Rates Likely to Moderate

Robinhood isn't able to provide much in the way of tangible guidance, given how volatile markets can be.

What they do provide is some vague outlook that the business should grow its revenues by at least double-digit.

However, I suspect that most investors have already expected and priced in this sort of growth rate in 2025, long ago.

Consequently, now the crucial question is this. Do double-digit growth rates translate into 10% y/y revenue growth rates? Or 15% y/y? Or potentially even as much as 25% y/y revenue growth rates?

Of course, it goes without saying, the stronger the growth rates the business delivers, the higher the multiple its stock will get.

The one blemish to think about here is that Q4 2024 saw particularly strong revenues, which will make this year's Q4 2025 up against a very challenging period.

But again, the main question is this, for the year as a whole, can investors get conviction that Robinhood could potentially grow at around 15% CAGR? Because if Robinhood manages to deliver around 15% topline growth today, the stock is already attractively priced.

HOOD Stock Valuation -- 26x Forward Free Cash Flow

As an Inflection investor, I'm drawn to the fact that Robinhood's balance sheet holds no debt. Case in point, even though there are approximately $3.5 billion of securities loaned, these are roughly balanced against its securities segregated on its balance sheet, under current assets.

Simply put, Robinhood holds about $5 billion of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet, with no debt. This means that more than 12% of its market cap is made up of cash, with no debt against that cash. This leaves Robinhood with the flexibility to either reinvest in its operations as it shows an inclination to do, or to repurchase its shares.

Moving on, Robinhood delivered about $1.2 billion of free cash flow in 2025. Thus, if we assume that together with approximately a 15% topline increase in its revenues this year, Robinhood's free cash flow increases by approximately 20% y/y, this would see Robinhood delivering about $1.5 billion of free cash flow in 2025.

This means that the stock is priced at 26x forward free cash flow. A figure that I believe to be very attractive, given its expected 15% topline growth this year, together with the fact that it holds no debt on its balance sheet.

Risk Factors to Consider

As alluded to earlier, the main question investors have to contend with here is what could be the fallout of interest rates on traders' volume.

Here's a quote from the earnings call that highlights this risk factor:

We are seeing that relationship, that natural hedge play out where rates go one direction and trading goes the other.

Another risk factor to keep in mind is that Robinhood's expansion into credit cards and lending which means more regulation and potential credit losses if customers don’t repay.

Their growth in lending, especially, has to be balanced carefully, with companies like SoFi Technologies, Inc. (SOFI) chasing similar users, and vying for market share too.

The Bottom Line

At the end of the day, I'm bullish on Robinhood because the financials just make sense.

The company is debt-free, sitting on about $5 billion in cash and securities, and it’s generating serious free cash flow—potentially $1.5 billion in 2025.

When I look at that and see it trading at just 26x forward free cash flow, for a company still growing its top line by double digits, it feels like a compelling setup.

There’s real value here if you’re willing to look past the short-term volatility.

data.title: Renesas Likely Meaningfully Undervalued, But Tariffs And Trade Chaos Create Risks
data.description:
The best I can say about the performance of leading Japanese semiconductor company Renesas Electronics (OTCPK:RNECY) (OTCPK:RNECF) (6723.T) is that it definitely could have been worse. Down about 15% since my last article (and 10% for the U.S. ADRs), Renesas did at least do better than the broader semi sector and specific competitors like Analog Devices (ADI), Infineon Technologies (OTCQX:IFNNY), Microchip Technology (MCHP), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), STMicroelectronics (STM), and Texas Instruments (TXN).

With market share gains, new products ramping, and an improved outlook for auto sales in Asian markets, as well as some improvement in industrial markets, there are some positives in Renesas’s outlook. On the other hand, U.S. tariffs have created exceptional chaos and may not only directly impact the price competitiveness of Renesas’s products (and their customers’ products), but also drive lower overall economic activity due to significantly greater uncertainty in the market.

If long-term revenue growth of 5% is still a valid expectation (and I believe it is), and Renesas can return its free cash flow margins to the low/mid-20%’s, the shares are likely 50% or more undervalued today, making this a name worth consideration for investors who can accept the above-average risk that is present today.

2025 Is Starting To Look More “Less Bad” Than “Better”

It’s no secret that the semiconductor industry had a tough 2024 (at least outside of direct data center/AI products), as end-user demand in major segments like autos and industrial weakened through the year. At the same time, both end-users and distributors worked down their inventories in response to not only soft immediate demand, but increased caution and uncertainty regarding a near-term recovery.

Overall microcontroller sales declined 22% in 2024, with an 11% decline in auto MCUs and a 47% decline MCUs for industrial applications. While not all of Renesas’s revenue comes from MCUs (it’s around 40% to 50% now), it’s still a significant part of the business, and it’s not as if analog or power semiconductor markets were remarkably better.

At this point, that expected and hoped-for recovery in 2025 continues sliding to the right. The trade and tariff policies of the U.S. administration have created significant uncertainty and turmoil in global markets, and while European economies were already looking at sluggish trends for 2025, the outlook for the U.S. is slipping as well.

I’m very interested to see what Renesas reports with respect to earnings, guidance, and management commentary when it reports first quarter 2025 results on April 24. I won’t be surprised if the company benefits from accelerated orders meant to get ahead of tariffs and posts stronger revenue for the quarter, but I likewise won’t be surprised if management is considerably more cautious with its guidance for the remainder of 2025.

Sales to auto customers are around half of Renesas’s revenue base, and while the outlook for passenger vehicle markets in Japan, China, and the rest of Asia are actually pretty healthy right now, the outlook for Europe and North America is weakening, with the imposition of tariffs likely to make affordability for U.S. customers a much more significant issue.

At the same time, quantifying the company’s total exposure to U.S. tariffs is challenging – while only about 10% to 15% of the company’s revenue comes from the U.S., I assume that a meaningful percentage of its sales to customers in Japan, China, and the rest of Asia are for products ultimately exported to the U.S. (Japanese carmakers, for instance, generate about one-third of their revenue from the U.S., with production spread across Japan, the U.S., Canada, and Mexico).

The industrial markets that Renesas serves are no easier to predict at this point. While Renesas has good leverage to factory automation, the ever-changing tariff outlook is likely to lead many customers to further postpone capex investments. Likewise, demand for chips used in products like appliances could weaken after an initial surge intended to get ahead of those tariffs, and I expect IoT product demand could be meaningfully impacted by tariffs as well given that not many of those are assembled in the U.S.

While it is certainly true that the U.S. market is only part of Renesas’s overall market opportunity, overall industrial capex is likely to remain depressed and companies are likely to remain cautious until there is more clarity on what the new “rules” are going to be.

Post-Chaos, There Are Still Growth Drivers

It may be more bullish than I realize to assume that there will be a new steady-state at some point (as opposed to years of ongoing chaos and unpredictability), but even so, I do think there are some solid internal positives for Renesas that position it favorably relative to many of its peers.

Renesas has continued to advance its system-on-a-chip capabilities for auto customers, with its 3nm R-Car SoC not only facilitating centralized electronic control units, but allowing automakers to take a “modular” approach to car design that allows multiple different products to be made with the same core components – in other words, the design of Renesas’s new chips allows OEMs to build economy, mid-range, and high-end vehicles from the same basic “backbone”, adding/upgrading components as desired for various price and performance ranges.

The company also continues to position itself as a leader in the emerging software-defined vehicle space. The company recently announced a multiyear development deal with Honda Motor Co. (HMC) to develop new high-performance SoCs for software-defined vehicles, and I expect the company’s leading-edge offerings and deep experience in auto SoCs to serve it well as the industry transitions to this approach.

I also continue to see opportunities outside the auto market. Renesas has been gaining some share in power semiconductors for data center servers, and I likewise see further growth opportunities in DDR5-related products like memory interfaces and power management chips. Longer term, automation and IoT remain meaningful opportunities for the company, particularly as it can offer high-end, high-performance MCUs and SoCs as well as complementary products like PMICs and memory interfaces.

The company is also doing alright from a share perspective. As per the most recent Gartner report, Renesas gained share in the MCU market in 2024. Although Infineon and NXP did better (gaining more than three points, and almost three points, respectively), Renesas nevertheless gained almost a point of share in 2024, bringing it up to almost 16% (versus 20% for Infineon, 19% for NXPI, 13% for STMicro, and 11% for Microchip).

Renesas still punches above its weight in auto MCUs (24% share versus 32% at Infineon and 21% at NXPI), but I believe at least some of the relative weakness in its industrial MCU business in 2024 (about 8% share, down almost two points) was due to particular customer and end-market exposures.

The Outlook

Weak demand from end-users and distributors has led to soft capacity utilization numbers at Renesas (around 35% in Q4 2024 and expected to be in the low-40%s in Q1 2025), but the company has nevertheless managed to maintain respectable margins. Non-GAAP gross margin of 56% in FY 2024 compares pretty well to peers (ranging from 39% at STM and 42% at Infineon to 58% at NXPI and Texas Instruments), and likewise with 29% non-GAAP operating margin (versus 35% at NXPI, 34% at TXN, 17% at Infineon, and 11% at STMicro).

Given my expectations for weak revenue in FY 2025, I don’t expect meaningful utilization or margin improvement, but if and when revenue recovers in FY 2026 and beyond, there should be meaningful margin leverage in the business and a return to low-to-mid-30%’s OPM in FY 2026 or FY 2027.

I’m expecting a slight (around 1%-1.5%) decline in revenue in FY 2025, and I’m about 3% below the Street at this point. I’m likewise below the Street in FY 2026 (rev up 11%), and I’m expecting long-term annualized growth around 5% against a trailing average closer to 6% (though that number includes M&A).

Although I’m modeling about a half-point of OPM erosion in FY 2025, I do think the company could maintain flat margins with a little more focus on operating efficiency (with that ever-present tension between maximizing near-term profitability while not compromising future growth). I’m expecting a return to 30%+ OPM in FY 2026, and I believe that the company can return to 20%-plus free cash flow margin in FY 2025 and grow FCF at a double-digit annualized rate from an admittedly low 2024 starting point.

Discounted cash flow suggests more than 50% undervaluation today, and I get a similar result with a near-term margin and multiples-based approach. If Renesas can achieve a 29% non-GAAP OPM in 2025, a 4.25x forward revenue multiple would be fair (if not a little conservative) based upon what the market has traditionally paid for similar profitability. That too supports 50%-plus upside, but it is worth mentioning that the market is often very sensitive to short-term outlooks, so I’m not surprised the current uncertainties over trade policy and global economic growth have the shares trading well below what would otherwise seem like a fair multiple.

The Bottom Line

I’m not going to argue that Renesas is the best-run semiconductor company out there (Analog Devices, NXP, and Texas Instruments all have very strong cases), but I do think it is a well-run company with good exposure to growth drivers like industrial automation and electrification, as well as vehicle digitalization and electrification. I do see elevated near-term risk as businesses around the world try to adjust to the new realities of U.S. policy, but Renesas is a diverse, well-run company and I think today’s valuation sets the stage for good long-term returns for investors willing to take on that elevated risk today.
